verses.explainer: Ampliatus, Paul's 'beloved in the Lord.' The name Ampliatus was extremely common among Roman slaves and freedpersons. If this Ampliatus was of slave origin, Paul's affectionate greeting illustrates the social leveling that occurred in the early church.

verses.text: Greet Urbanus, our fellow worker in Christ, and Stachys, my beloved.
verses.explainer: Urbanus, a fellow worker in Christ, and Stachys, Paul's beloved. Two more names from the Roman congregation. 'Urbanus' means 'city dweller' and was common in Rome.

verses.text: Greet Apelles, the approved in Christ. Greet those who are of the household of Aristobulus.
verses.explainer: Apelles, 'approved in Christ' -- a man whose faith has been tested and proven. And greetings to those belonging to the household of Aristobulus, which may refer to the household of the grandson of Herod the Great.

verses.text: Greet Herodion, my kinsman. Greet them of the household of Narcissus, who are in the Lord.
verses.explainer: Herodion, Paul's kinsman (a fellow Jew), and those of the household of Narcissus who are in the Lord. Narcissus may be the famous freedman of Emperor Claudius, whose household slaves would have passed to Nero after Narcissus's death.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Household of Narcissus', 'explanation': "If this is Narcissus the freedman of Claudius, his household included hundreds of slaves who became imperial property after his forced suicide in 54 AD. Paul greets 'those in the Lord' among them -- Christians within the imperial household itself. This would confirm Philippians 4:22, where Paul sends greetings from 'those of Caesar's household.'"}

verses.text: Greet Tryphaena and Tryphosa, who labor in the Lord. Greet Persis, the beloved, who labored much in the Lord.
verses.explainer: Three more women who 'labor in the Lord': Tryphaena, Tryphosa (possibly sisters, given their similar names), and Persis, 'the beloved,' who 'labored much.' The same strong verb (kopiao) Paul uses for apostolic ministry is applied to these women.

verses.text: Greet Rufus, the chosen in the Lord, and his mother and mine.
verses.explainer: Rufus, 'chosen in the Lord,' and his mother, who has been a mother to Paul as well. If this is the Rufus mentioned in Mark 15:21, he was the son of Simon of Cyrene, who carried Jesus' cross.

verses.text:
Greet Asyncritus, Phlegon, Hermes, Patrobas, Hermas, and the brothers
 who are with them.
verses.explainer: Asyncritus, Phlegon, Hermes, Patrobas, Hermas, and the brothers with them -- likely another house church grouping. Several of these names were common among slaves and freedpersons.

verses.text: Greet Philologus and Julia, Nereus and his sister, and Olympas, and all the saints who are with them.
verses.explainer: Philologus and Julia, Nereus and his sister, and Olympas, and all the saints with them -- yet another house church. The pairing of Philologus and Julia suggests a married couple hosting a congregation.

verses.text: Greet one another with a holy kiss. The assemblies of Christ greet you.
verses.explainer: Greet one another with a holy kiss. All the churches of Christ greet you. The holy kiss was a standard form of greeting in early Christian worship -- a physical expression of spiritual kinship that crossed social boundaries.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'The holy kiss', 'explanation': "The kiss of greeting (philema hagion) was practiced in early Christian assemblies as a sign of unity and mutual love (1 Corinthians 16:20, 2 Corinthians 13:12, 1 Thessalonians 5:26). It crossed boundaries of gender, ethnicity, and social class that Roman culture carefully maintained. By the second century, the practice was regulated to prevent scandal -- men kissed men, women kissed women. Eventually it evolved into the 'kiss of peace' still practiced in some liturgies."}

verses.text: Now I beg you, brothers, look out for those who are causing the divisions and occasions of stumbling, contrary to the doctrine which you learned, and turn away from them.
verses.explainer: Paul inserts an unexpected and sharp warning: watch out for those who cause divisions and put obstacles in your way, contrary to the teaching you have learned. Avoid them. After twenty-six warm greetings, this sudden severity suggests Paul has specific troublemakers in mind.

thenVsNow.then: Traveling teachers who undermined local church unity were a recurring problem in the early church. Paul confronted similar issues in Galatia and Corinth. The divisive teachers may have been Judaizers demanding Torah observance, or possibly antinomians promoting moral laxity.
thenVsNow.now: Paul's combination of warm fellowship and sharp doctrinal vigilance offers a model for churches today. Authentic community does not mean tolerating teachings that undermine the gospel. Love and truth are not opposites but partners.

verses.text: For those who are such don’t serve our Lord, Jesus Christ, but their own belly; and by their smooth and flattering speech, they deceive the hearts of the innocent.
verses.explainer: These people do not serve our Lord Christ but their own appetites. By smooth talk and flattery they deceive the hearts of the naive. Paul exposes the method: the divisive teachers sound appealing but serve themselves, not Christ.

verses.text: For your obedience has become known to all. I rejoice therefore over you. But I desire to have you wise in that which is good, but innocent in that which is evil.
verses.explainer: Everyone has heard about the Romans' obedience, and Paul rejoices over them. He wants them to be wise about what is good and innocent about what is evil. Wisdom does not require experience with evil -- a wise person can be naive about wickedness and still be wise.

verses.text:
And the God of peace will quickly crush Satan under your feet.
The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you.
verses.explainer: The God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet. The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you. A startling promise that echoes Genesis 3:15 -- the first prophecy that the serpent's head would be crushed. Paul sees the ultimate defeat of evil as imminent.

verses.text: Timothy, my fellow worker, greets you, as do Lucius, Jason, and Sosipater, my relatives.
verses.explainer: Timothy, Paul's most trusted associate, sends greetings along with Lucius, Jason, and Sosipater -- Paul's relatives. Timothy appears more frequently in Paul's letters than any other co-worker.

verses.text: I, Tertius, who write the letter, greet you in the Lord.
verses.explainer: I, Tertius, who wrote this letter, greet you in the Lord. A remarkable moment: the scribe interrupts to send his own greeting. Paul dictated Romans, and Tertius physically wrote it. This is the only place in the New Testament where a scribe identifies himself by name.

thenVsNow.then: Letter writing in antiquity commonly involved dictation to a trained scribe (amanuensis). The scribe might copy word-for-word or be given latitude to compose in their own style. Tertius's personal greeting suggests he was a fellow believer, not a hired professional.
thenVsNow.now: Tertius's interjection reminds us that the New Testament was produced by real human beings in real historical circumstances. The doctrine of inspiration does not erase the human element -- it works through it.

verses.text: Gaius, my host and host of the whole assembly, greets you. Erastus, the treasurer of the city, greets you, as does Quartus, the brother.
verses.explainer: Gaius, Paul's host and host of the whole church, sends greetings. Erastus, the city treasurer, and Quartus send greetings. These details reveal the social range of the early church -- from slaves to city officials.

verses.text: The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all! Amen.
verses.explainer: The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all. Amen. A final benediction of grace. Some manuscripts omit this verse, but it fittingly closes the letter with the same grace that opened it (1:7).

Some manuscripts include a final doxology here (others place it at 14:24-26). If original to this location, it echoes the great themes of Romans: the gospel, the mystery long hidden and now revealed, the obedience of faith among all nations, and glory to God through Jesus Christ forever.
